summary: A computational account of how factorizing graph-structured representations lets an agent recombine and reuse previously learned experience compositionally, presented at Cognitive Computational Neuroscience 2023.
Reports that single neurons in human prefrontal cortex encode economic risk and risk prediction errors during value-based decision-making, presented at Cognitive Computational Neuroscience 2023.

Introduces CBT, a correlation-based transfer loss that constrains corresponding convolutional kernels in two CNNs to respond similarly in similar contexts, carrying semantic feature structure from a source to a target domain for unsupervised domain adaptation.

Reframes RL actions as a sequence of internal choices under an action model, augmenting the MDP with an information-theoretic notion of internal action so that large pretrained sequence models can serve as priors for multi-task reinforcement learning.

GeoSumm performs unsupervised extractive opinion summarization: an encoder-decoder learns topical text representations via dictionary learning across decoder layers, then scores sentence importance with an approximate geodesic distance to extract popular and aspect-specific opinions.

A multi-agent image-reference game where a speaker (CLIP vision encoder plus an LLM adapter) is rewarded for describing a target so one listener identifies it but another cannot. RL fine-tuning yields audience-specific language specialization from rewards alone, transferring zero-shot to real images.

A position paper contending that language-model pre-training research accrues scientific debt through insufficient methodological rigour, and arguing for more careful, controlled experimentation in pre-training studies.

Shows that initializing the bias of a language generation model's final output layer to the log of token unigram frequencies provides a useful natural bias, accelerating convergence and improving generation quality.
A book-chapter review of machine learning for connectomics, surveying methods from representation learning of neural-imaging data through to model fitting.

Argues stylistic rewriting should account for surrounding context rather than operate sentence-by-sentence. Introduces CtxSimFit, combining source similarity with contextual cohesiveness; humans prefer contextual rewrites, which sentence-level metrics track poorly but CtxSimFit and context-infused metrics capture well.

Improves multi-hop question answering in pretrained LMs using soft prompts that map multi-hop questions to random-walk paths over structured knowledge graphs, chaining stored facts toward the answer. On two T5 models this substantially beats standard tuning.

An fMRI study of English and Mandarin speakers comprehending object-extracted relative clauses finds a shared left-hemisphere network across both languages, plus some additional temporal-lobe activity specific to Chinese.

Studies decompounding (splitting compound words) at scale, releasing a 255k-word dataset spanning 56 languages. LLMs perform poorly; dedicated self-supervised models beat prior unsupervised tools by 13.9% accuracy, and are used to build the CompoundPiece subword tokenizer.

A chapter on many-objective (four-plus) black-box optimization combining Bayesian optimization with Gaussian processes and game-theoretic solution concepts such as Nash equilibria and Kalai-Smorodinsky solutions, with corresponding algorithms and illustrative results.

Uses large-scale transliteration via the UROMAN tool to adapt multilingual pretrained LMs to low-resource and unseen-script languages. Comparing data- and parameter-efficient strategies across 14 languages, romanization helps most for unseen scripts and limited data, sometimes beating non-transliteration methods.

An empirical comparison of few-shot tool-assisted LM generation strategies finds strong no-tool baselines remain competitive, that refining incorrect outputs with tools beats retrieving before or during generation, and that tool use costs orders of magnitude more tokens for little gain.

Extends the continuous cellular automaton Lenia with mass conservation, enabling update-rule parameters to be embedded in the state (parameter localization) so multiple species coexist and evolve, supporting open-ended evolutionary dynamics.

Uses genetic programming to evolve interpretable agent logic for agent-based models from domain-independent primitives like basic math operators rather than domain-specific ones, recovering symbolic models of flocking and opinion dynamics that match reference behavior, generalize well, and resist noise.

Re-examines exact-match schema linking (EMSL) in text-to-SQL, showing it reduces robustness to synonyms and typos. A pretrained-LM encoder without EMSL is more robust, and the authors add grammar linking to align question terms with SQL keywords.

Proposes a realistic end-to-end domain-shift evaluation for open-domain QA over five domains, finding models fail to generalize and high retrieval scores still yield poor answers. Shift-matched data-augmentation interventions raise end-to-end performance by up to about 24 points.

Proposes a lenient character-error-rate metric for Japanese ASR that credits valid alternative spellings, building a lattice of plausible respellings from lexical resources and an NMT kanji reconstructor. Raters deemed 95.4% of variants plausible; CER falls 2.4-3.1% absolute.

Replaces standard codecs like JPEG with neural compression and feeds the compressed representation directly into ordinary video networks. This improves data-transfer, memory, and speed at every stage, enabling training on hour-long videos with hardware previously limited to seconds-long clips.

Surveys grounded-language settings and pragmatic modeling approaches, analyzing how task goals, environmental context, and communicative affordances enrich meaning beyond the literal, and recommends grounded task designs that better elicit pragmatic phenomena.

Introduces DRIP, a backward-reachability method certifying collision avoidance for systems under neural-network control policies. A refinement loop over the relaxation domain and closed-form polytope bounds tighten backprojection-set estimates versus prior linear-program and hyper-rectangle approaches.

Cross-dataset evaluation of pretrained vision-and-language models on VQA reveals poor out-of-distribution generalization, indicating models learn the benchmark rather than the task. Generative models prove more robust than discriminative ones, and standard VQA metrics over-penalize correct answers.

Introduces a transformer that learns object-centric summary vectors for video, fusing visual and spatio-temporal trajectory cues with a trajectory-contrast loss. The object-centric representation transfers better than prior video representations to unseen objects, unseen environments, and tasks beyond action classification.

Identifies three ways learned reward functions misassign high reward in RL-trained conditional text generation: noise-induced spurious correlation, naturally occurring spurious correlation, and covariate shift. These undesirable patterns get amplified during RL; the paper discusses concrete examples and possible fixes.

CRoW is a manually curated multi-task benchmark probing commonsense reasoning across six real-world NLP tasks, built by rewriting examples with commonsense-violating perturbations. It exposes a large model-human gap spanning physical, temporal, and social commonsense.

Studies attribution, whether an answer is supported by its cited sources, in cross-lingual question answering where question and evidence differ in language. Proposes evaluation methods and modeling approaches to measure and improve cross-lingual answer attribution.

Explores masked-language-model pretraining variants for code-switched text that respect language boundaries before masking, with token language identity from annotators, classifiers, or frequency estimates, plus a residual connection from an earlier layer. Gains reach 5.8 F1 (QA) and 2.7 F1 (sentiment).
